class="content-reactions_count-wrapper"><button class="icon-button reactions-button reactions-count-button reactions-hover-button" data-native-bridge="true"></button><div class="reactions-count-popup content-reactions-popup"></div></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-loading"><div class="reactions-skeleton-left"><div class="reactions-skeleton-el share-skeleton"></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-el"></div></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-right"><div class="reactions-skeleton-el"></div></div></div></div><p><strong>MARCUS SMART WAS</strong> scanning Anthony Edwards' shooting line in the box score as he slumped in a chair in front of his locker. It was March 10, moments after the <a data-clubhouse-guid="2876e98b-b9bc-2920-4319-46e6943f8be4" href="/nba/team/_/name/lal/los-angeles-lakers">Los Angeles Lakers</a> had defeated the <a data-clubhouse-guid="13f727cb-254d-b484-a337-93fcc0047add" href="/nba/team/_/name/min/minnesota-timberwolves">Minnesota Timberwolves</a> 120-106, jumping from fifth in the Western Conference playoff race to third.</p><p>As Smart reviewed it line by line, he suddenly perked up, his exhaustion turning to something else: a sparkle of satisfaction.</p><p>Edwards, the Minnesota Timberwolves' 24-year-old superstar, had scored just 14 points and went 2-for-15 from the floor -- including 0-for-5 in the 23 possessions Smart guarded him.</p><p>The same Edwards who tormented the Lakers' wing defenders last spring on his way to averaging 26.8 points in the lower-seeded Wolves' five-game gentleman's sweep of L.A. had been stifled by Smart, the former Defensive Player of the Year who joined the purple and gold on a discount deal months after that first-round upset.</p><p>While the win represented a glimmer of hope for this year's Lakers squad that so far has displayed too much inconsistency on both ends to be feared as a top contender in the coming playoffs, it meant so much more for Smart.</p><p>He wasn't supposed to still be locking up All-Stars at age 32.</p><p>Not after his last two injury-riddled, irrelevant seasons in Basketball Siberia.</p><aside class="inline editorial float-r" data-behavior="article_related"><h2 class="editorial-title">Editor's Picks</h2><ul><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48211868/lakers-reaves-doncic-deliver-late-game-magic-ot-thriller" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0315/r1628634_1296x1296_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48211868/lakers-reaves-doncic-deliver-late-game-magic-ot-thriller">Reaves, Luka deliver late-game magic in OT thriller</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Dave McMenamin</span></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48216797/reaves-was-stripped-clean-late-lakers-win-report-says" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0315/r1628915_683x683_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48216797/reaves-was-stripped-clean-late-lakers-win-report-says">NBA: Missed call late in Lakers-Nuggets thriller</a><div class="editorial--metadata"></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48155517/nba-2025-2026-alternate-reality-kevin-durant-2021-eastern-conference-giannis-kyrie-irving" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0309/nba_kd_alternate_reality2_cr_1x1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48155517/nba-2025-2026-alternate-reality-kevin-durant-2021-eastern-conference-giannis-kyrie-irving">How the NBA would've changed if KD's sneaker were smaller: An alternate timeline with new champs and wild trades</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Zach Kram</span></div></h2></li></ul><div class="button button-alt sm btn-related">2 Related</div></aside><p>He played 20 games for a 27-win <a data-clubhouse-guid="af5d4942-aeb5-2d07-2a8a-f70b54617e51" href="/nba/team/_/name/mem/memphis-grizzlies">Memphis Grizzlies</a> team in 2023-2024 and 15 games for an 18-win <a data-clubhouse-guid="64d73af6-b8ec-e213-87e8-a4eab3a692e7" href="/nba/team/_/name/wsh/washington-wizards">Washington Wizards</a> squad a year ago, while his former team, the <a data-clubhouse-guid="2ca761df-5f60-b2e9-22ed-e099c46d889b" href="/nba/team/_/name/bos/boston-celtics">Boston Celtics</a>, soared to a title without him.</p><p>And he certainly wasn't supposed to be holding on to the stat sheet with his right hand, which he had come dangerously close to losing eight years ago.</p><p>All of which set up Smart's attitude for this season -- one he has pushed the Lakers' star trio of <a data-player-guid="1f6592b3-ff53-d321-8dc5-6038d48c1786" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/1966/lebron-james">LeBron James</a>, <a data-player-guid="583794eb-0f38-9bbd-3e25-9dd33b7f83b8" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3945274/luka-doncic">Luka Doncic</a> and <a data-player-guid="8a9d9e1c-6174-0009-4883-3b4e16b492d0" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4066457/austin-reaves">Austin Reaves</a>, and the rest of the roster, to adopt: If he can change the trajectory at this stage of his career, after what he has been through, who is to say the Lakers can't change theirs for the last couple months of the season?</p><p>"We're tired of hearing people talk s---, basically," Smart told ESPN. "I know I am. And if you're a competitor, if you have any type of competitor in you, you're going to be tired of that too. So you want to try to prove 'em wrong."</p><hr><p><strong>DESPITE PLAYING 56</strong> games for the Lakers so far, and leading them with a team-best plus-minus of plus-209, the most consequential shot of Smart's career in L.A. still came as a visitor.</p><p>It was Jan. 23, 2018, and the Celtics, then the No. 1 team in the East, were on the road to play the Lakers, then the No. 11 team in the West.</p><p>Former Lakers guard <a data-player-guid="171eb159-a032-0148-a7ab-946354f6991a" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/2581018/kentavious-caldwell-pope">Kentavious Caldwell-Pope</a> had gone to the line with 5.7 seconds remaining and L.A. up by one and missed both free throws, with the second clanking off the rim and into Smart's hands.</p><p>Smart, who already had 22 points in the game, took the rebound and dribbled frantically up the court until he got to the 3-point line, where he pulled up for 3 at the buzzer.</p><p>His shot hit the front iron and rimmed out, and the Celtics lost the game 108-107.</p><p>Later that night, at the team hotel, he replayed the shot in his mind and frustration boiled over.</p><p>He punched at a picture frame in his room and, this time, he didn't miss, shattering the glass and leaving a 5-inch shard wedged inside the flesh of his palm.</p><aside class="inline float-r inline-track"><h2 class="img-title">Upcoming NBA games on ESPN/ABC</h2><img data-lazyload="true" data-src="https://a.espncdn.com/photo/2024/0725/r1362869_800x450_16-9.jpg" class="round lazyload"><p><strong>Wednesday, March 18</strong><br />
<a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/game/_/gameId/401810854/warriors-celtics" target="_blank">Warriors at Celtics, 7 p.m. ET</a><br />
<a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/game/_/gameId/401810862/lakers-rockets" target="_blank">Lakers at Rockets, 9:30 p.m. ET</a></p><p><strong>Wednesday, March 25</strong><br />
<a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/game/_/gameId/401810906/hawks-pistons" target="_blank">Hawks at Pistons, 7 p.m. ET</a><br />
<a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/game/_/gameId/401810913/rockets-timberwolves" target="_blank">Rockets at Timberwolves, 9:30 p.m. ET</a></p>
</aside><p>"I got rushed to the ER and lost a lot of blood," Smart told ESPN. "I passed out. ... That's how much I lost."</p><p>When he regained consciousness, after receiving 20 stitches, Smart realized the severity of his wound.</p><p>"The doctor looked at me in my eye and told me, 'I don't know how you still have use of your right hand,'" Smart said. "'You should honestly be thanking God every day.'" The piece of glass that had sliced into his hand was just millimeters from severing its use entirely. "They said it laid perfectly in between every tendon in my hand without damaging anything," Smart said. "And they had to leave the extra piece in, because they said it will cause more damage if we go get it out."</p><p>Smart missed 11 games before he was back on the court, helping Boston reach Game 7 of the Eastern Conference finals that season before losing to James and the <a data-clubhouse-guid="ec79ad1f-e6d2-7762-a2db-7fe97d35126b" href="/nba/team/_/name/cle/cleveland-cavaliers">Cleveland Cavaliers</a>.</p><p>The Celtics reached the postseason in all nine seasons Smart was there, including a run to the 2022 NBA Finals to cap his DPOY campaign, before they lost in six games to the final glory run of the Golden State Warriors' dynasty.</p><p>That next year, Boston lost to the eighth-seeded <a data-clubhouse-guid="81e3212c-30ef-9b1b-5edb-453b13ff265a" href="/nba/team/_/name/mia/miami-heat">Miami Heat</a> in the 2023 conference finals, and the team retooled, sending Smart to Memphis in a three-team deal that landed <a data-player-guid="a1f467e2-5304-c8c1-db23-80fe313c3d7c" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3102531/kristaps-porzingis">Kristaps Porzingis</a> on the Celtics.</p><p>It was there, in Memphis, that Smart began to lose his grip -- figuratively and literally -- on his career.</p><aside class="inline inline-photo full"><figure><picture ><source data-srcset="https://a2.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=%2Fphoto%2F2026%2F0315%2Fr1628871_1296x729_16%2D9.jpg&w=570&format=jpg, https://a2.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=%2Fphoto%2F2026%2F0315%2Fr1628871_1296x729_16%2D9.jpg&w=1140&cquality=40&format=jpg 2x" media="(min-width: 376px)"><source data-srcset="https://a2.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=%2Fphoto%2F2026%2F0315%2Fr1628871_1296x729_16%2D9.jpg&w=375, https://a2.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=%2Fphoto%2F2026%2F0315%2Fr1628871_1296x729_16%2D9.jpg&w=750&cquality=40&format=jpg 2x" media="(max-width: 375px)"><img  class=" lazyload lazyload" data-image-container=".inline-photo" height=320 width=570 ></picture><figcaption class="photoCaption"><cite>Nick Tomoyasu/NBAE via Getty Images</cite></figcaption></figure></aside><p><strong>SMART'S RIGHT HAND</strong> fuels his most important skill: his anticipation on defense. He has amassed more than 1,000 steals in his career by sticking his hands where they don't belong. And it only became more mangled in Memphis.</p><p>In January 2024, he ruptured a tendon in his proximal interphalangeal joint -- where the finger bends -- ending his season.</p><p>"I've had two dislocations with torn ligaments in two of the fingers," Smart said. "I've had glass in my hand. I've torn ligaments on my right thumb and had surgery there. I dislocated four out of my five fingers in total ... my whole right hand just has been through a lot. So to be honest, I'm blessed to even have my right hand.</p><p>"For six years after the incident with the glass, I still had glass in my hand and I played with it. And there would be times where because of that, my hand would go numb. A lot of times, a lot of games, I couldn't control it. I had to play and there were a lot of times when I'm shooting the ball and just, I had no feeling in the arm, the hand."</p><p>Meanwhile, Memphis had bigger problems. <a data-player-guid="de5f87e0-8571-a730-ade4-6d00d10b8577" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4279888/ja-morant">Ja Morant</a>, suspended for the first 25 games of 2023-24, went on to play just nine games, leading a roster so riddled with injuries that 33 different players suited up for the team at some point that season.</p><p>The Grizzlies missed the playoffs -- the first time in Smart's career he was on a team that failed to qualify for the postseason -- while his former team went back to the Finals to face Doncic and the <a data-clubhouse-guid="f00d1f4e-4ce6-d581-466c-5b52531cf7ad" href="/nba/team/_/name/dal/dallas-mavericks">Dallas Mavericks</a>. "I was watching every bit of it," Smart said. "Does it hurt? Of course it hurts because you were the one there from the start before 2014, when Avery Bradley was still there, you guys were in the rebuild and so you watched everything grow. You were there when the seed was planted and everything. So it definitely hurt."</p><p>The start to his next season in Memphis was even harder.</p><p>Over the team's first five games, Smart averaged just 5.6 points on 23.5% shooting (16.7% from 3), and the Grizzlies stumbled out to a 2-3 start.</p><p>After an ankle injury sidelined him for two weeks, Smart returned and Memphis went 10-2 in its next 12 games before he suffered a partial tear of the proximal extensor hood of his right index finger.</p><p>Around this time, Smart says, he felt a shift in how the Grizzlies organization treated him.</p><p>He says he felt pressured to play through it.</p><p>"I wanted to be 100%, to give everything I got," Smart said. "As I'm still working out and getting ready, they're just like, 'No, your doctor said you're ready now.' And I'm like, 'I hear what my doctor said, but ... it's how I feel. Yes, surgically the finger is fine. But physically, no. The finger is still weak. I'm still strengthening it. This is my dominant hand.' So, they didn't want to hear it and they just kept saying, 'You need to play.'"</p><p>Furthermore, during his rehab, Smart heard "a rumor going around that people said I don't want to be here," he said, despite actions that would suggest the contrary -- from giving customized boxing gloves to his teammates, to his wife inviting the roster to his birthday party, to writing personalized holiday cards and stuffing them with gift cards for all the staff.</p><p>After a 21-game absence, he returned to the lineup Feb. 5, 2025, on the road against the <a data-clubhouse-guid="5a9c33b8-63fd-ff34-a833-925fe89320a6" href="/nba/team/_/name/tor/toronto-raptors">Toronto Raptors</a>.</p><p>His hand still didn't feel right -- he went 0-for-6 -- but Memphis outscored Toronto by five points in the 18 minutes he was on the floor in the win.</p><p>What happened next made him feel like the "scapegoat" for a franchise in flux, Smart said. The following day, he was traded to Washington.</p><p>When reached by ESPN about Smart's claims, the Grizzlies had no comment.</p><aside class="inline float-r inline-track"><h2 class="img-title">'The Hoop Collective'</h2><img data-lazyload="true" data-src="https://a.espncdn.com/photo/2025/1112/r1574751_800x320_5-2.jpg" class="round lazyload"><p>"The Hoop Collective" podcast, hosted by Brian Windhorst, releases new shows every Monday, Wednesday and Friday during the NBA season.<br />
• <strong><a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/story/_/id/47168192/the-hoop-collective-latest-episodes-brian-windhorst-nba-podcast-archive-best-quotes">Watch/listen to the latest episodes</a></strong></p>
</aside><p>The deal was part of a three-team trade with the <a data-clubhouse-guid="e266c4ea-9af3-1bd8-06fb-fd608fd30e26" href="/nba/team/_/name/sac/sacramento-kings">Sacramento Kings</a>. Memphis had to include a first-round pick in what essentially became a salary dump, to get off the remainder of Smart's $20.2 million contract for 2024-25 and $21.3 million for the following season.</p><p>The maneuver allowed Memphis to avoid luxury tax implications with its planned extension for <a data-player-guid="dbe0e9e5-0046-c414-000b-f4e629b08f5d" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4277961/jaren-jackson-jr">Jaren Jackson Jr</a>.</p><p>"The Marcus that you got intel on from Memphis was probably not as consistent with the Marcus that he was in Boston and in his entire career," a Wizards source told ESPN.</p><p>"I think a lot of things went into that. But at the core, we knew who he was as a person and felt really comfortable adding him to a group. And he exceeded expectations in the short amount of time he was with us."</p><p>Smart credits the Wizards for how accommodating they were to him during the 2½ months he spent with the team.</p><p>"Washington was great," Smart said. "I just had to get healthy. ... That was my main thing."</p><p>Smart reached a buyout agreement with the Wizards in July to become a free agent, giving back $6.5 million of his 2025-26 contract according to ESPN's NBA front office insider Bobby Marks.</p><p>And then the Lakers came calling.</p><hr><p><strong>L.A. HAD LIMITED</strong> options to upgrade its roster coming off that playoff loss to the Wolves.</p><p>Outside of trading a future first-round pick, the Lakers' only avenue to reshape their roster was opening up the $14.1 million non-taxpayer midlevel exception by not re-signing <a data-player-guid="e05f4c3a-a0a6-e87f-d752-a5c092b7f474" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/2578185/dorian-finney-smith">Dorian Finney-Smith</a>, plus the biannual exception.</p><p><a data-player-guid="9af41ea8-a24c-025f-a63f-8263fbf0ca66" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4278129/deandre-ayton">Deandre Ayton</a> and <a data-player-guid="a2ffece1-7bf0-3c54-aa59-b150b6ecd315" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4592691/jake-laravia">Jake LaRavia</a> split the MLE, and Smart -- who was recruited with a phone call by Doncic -- got the biannual, agreeing to a two-year, $11 million deal, with a player option on the second year.</p><p>He says he wanted to prove that despite being 12 years into his career, his last two seasons were anomalies, not a downward trend.</p><p>When he arrived in L.A., he had a conversation with coach JJ Redick about the role he hoped to occupy.</p><p>Redick recalls the conversation.</p><p>He was at a Fini Pizza in Amagansett, in the Hamptons, and left the restaurant to take the call.</p><p>"I told JJ, 'I'm going to go out here and give you everything I got,'" Smart said. "'I just ask that if I do earn it, then let me have it. Don't play too many politics.' And that's all I asked, and that's all you can ask."</p><p>Redick said there were three things he wanted to get across to Smart, now that he had cleared waivers from Washington and was deciding on his next destination. "It was, 'We need you. We need your defense. We need your voice,'" Redick said. "The second part of that was, 'I know you because I've played against you and I've competed against you. ... You're at your best when you're playing for something. If you come here, we can be playing for something.' And he agreed with that."</p><p>And the third? "'You're going to play,'" Redick said he told Smart. "'I don't know exactly what that looks like, but I'm very confident that if you're at your best, you're going to play a lot.'"</p><p>Redick kept his word, starting him 49 games as Smart has averaged 9.6 points, 2.8 rebounds, 2.8 assists and 1.4 steals per game, and living with his 40.3% shooting from the floor and 34.0% from 3 in large part because of the Lakers' 111.1 defensive efficiency with Smart on the floor. It's 117.7 with him off this season.</p><p>Smart has drawn 19 charges this season, tied for the second most in the NBA, which is already more than the 16 he drew during his DPOY campaign -- and there's still a month to go in the regular season.</p><p>"He competes every night," Reaves told ESPN. "It's not always pretty, but his competitive level's going to be at an all-time high every single night. And you need guys like that. I think he's the one that sets the example for us on the court to go out there and play as hard as we possibly can, because you know he's going to do that.</p><p>"When he's doing that, every single night flying around, it makes you be accountable to the effort that you bring."</p><p><strong>"WHO TOLD YOU</strong> to miss it?" a wide-eyed Smart asked Reaves.</p><p>On Saturday, moments after the Lakers had defeated the <a data-clubhouse-guid="c4aceb39-0eb9-a30b-1120-9cb5b12b677a" href="/nba/team/_/name/den/denver-nuggets">Denver Nuggets</a> 127-125 in overtime, Smart didn't want to talk about his strip of <a data-player-guid="d6c22a9f-fbe3-1566-e175-c86137d057a5" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3064290/aaron-gordon">Aaron Gordon</a> that set up a crucial layup with less than a minute to go in the fourth quarter, one of his five steals.</p><p>He didn't want to discuss the go-ahead 3 he hit with 30 seconds to go in OT that put the Lakers up two.</p><p>He didn't even want to yap about his part in holding a notorious Lakers killer, <a data-player-guid="153b92a9-8489-74e3-c524-6527eb991e84" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3936299/jamal-murray">Jamal Murray</a>, to five points on 1-for-14 shooting.</p><p>What had Smart excited was his part in the strategy, suggesting to Reaves that, when he went to the foul line for two shots with 5.2 seconds left in the fourth and the Lakers trailing by three, he intentionally miss the second one.</p><p>Reaves ended up executing the miss to perfection and L.A. held on in the extra session to sustain its late-season inertia.</p><p>"I feel like every game," Doncic said of Smart after the Denver victory, "he does something different to help us win."</p><p>And his defensive commitment has remained a constant.</p><p>Smart has held 2026 All-Stars, like Murray and Edwards, to a 44.3% effective field goal percentage when he has guarded them, fifth best among 75 players to defend 100-plus shots this season, according to ESPN Research.</p><aside class="inline float-r inline-track"><h2 class="img-title">Breaking News from Shams Charania</h2><p><a href="https://www.espn.com/contributor/shams-charania" target="_blank"><img alt="" class="floatright" alt="" class="imageLoaded" src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/i/columnists/full/charania_shams.png" width="40%"></a></p><p>Download the ESPN app and enable <a href="https://www.espn.com/contributor/shams-charania" target="new">Shams Charania's news alerts</a> to receive push notifications for the latest updates first. Opt in by tapping the alerts bell in the top right corner. For more information, <a href="https://www.espn.com/espn/story/_/id/43511398/introducing-espn-reporter-breaking-news-alerts-faq-fans" target="new">click here</a>.</p>
</aside><p>"He brings a grittiness and a point-of-attack defender that maybe they didn't have a year ago," said Wolves coach Chris Finch. "A guy that can mark somebody all by himself, without a ton of help. Which really is huge, particularly in the playoffs."</p><p>And on a team with Reaves, Doncic and James staying in the headlines, Smart has made his noise in the locker room, as a needed voice.</p><p>"On this team, we got a lot of egos, which isn't saying that these egos are out of control or bad. It's just everybody's different," Smart said of his approach. "We have different personalities and sometimes they just don't mix at the right time. It's all about timing with everything.</p><p>"And sometimes you might not feel good this day or you might be having a bad game or bad couple games and it just doesn't feel right. So for me, my leadership was to just make sure I can keep the guys together, keep the positivity and not really let us go into that sunken place that you see most teams do when they're in a drought."</p><p>There have been some tough stretches for Smart this season, too -- just like for the team. He has missed games with back spasms. He went 1-for-12 in a loss to the LA Clippers, 2-for-9 in a loss to the <a data-clubhouse-guid="61719eb2-11c3-4e3d-90c3-0a1319fd850b" href="/nba/team/_/name/ny/new-york-knicks">New York Knicks</a>, 0-for-7 in a loss to Boston.</p><p>Still, he has kept at it. On March 3, with L.A. clinging to a four-point lead in the final minutes of the fourth against the <a data-clubhouse-guid="9461f397-7882-94c0-c18c-e89bdc9e570e" href="/nba/team/_/name/no/new-orleans-pelicans">New Orleans Pelicans</a>, Smart received a pass from Doncic in the corner and hit the 3 to go up seven with 1:34 to go, sealing it -- in a game in which he also had seven assists, four steals and three blocks.</p><div class="ad-300"><div class="ad-center"><div class="ad-slot ad-slot-incontent2 ad-wrapper" data-slot-type="incontent2" data-slot-kvps="pos=incontent2" data-independent="true"></div></div></div><p>"To have the trust to say, 'You know what? I'm going to give it to this guy in the corner right here,' I definitely think that it stems from the blocks, the steals, the energy that I bring," Smart said. "Knowing that this guy's working. And let's reward him."</p><p>He has had the third-most starts,  fifth-most minutes and sixth-most shot attempts this season for the Lakers. He has had a hand in everything they do.</p><p>"He's made an impact on winning," Redick said. "And I think that ultimately is ... that's how you rewrite the narrative of your career, is if you're on a winning team."</p><p><em>ESPN's Matt Williams contributed to this report.</em></p>

data-slot-type="instream" data-slot-kvps="pos=instream"></div><header class="article-header"><h1>SGA breaks Wilt's record: Thunder star's historic feat, by the numbers</h1></header><div class="article-body"><div class="article-meta"><div class="byline-wrap"><ul class="authors byline-authors"><li class="single-author mugshot-shown"><div class="author-img"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/i/columnists/full/kram_zach.png&h=80&w=80&scale=crop" alt="" width="40" height="40"></div><div class="author has-bio">Zach Kram<span class="timestamp ">Mar 12, 2026, 11:07 PM ET</span></div><div class="author-overlay"><a class="icon-font-after icon-close-solid-after" href="#">Close</a><ul>Zach Kram is a national NBA writer for ESPN.com, specializing in Authors</div></div></div><div class="content-reactions reactions-allowed " data-behavior="content_reactions" data-contentid="48165108" data-nowid="1-48165108" data-contentTitle="SGA breaks Wilt's record: Thunder star's historic feat, by the numbers"><div class="content-reactions-popup-overlay"></div><div class="content-reactions_reactions-wrapper"><div class="share-button-wrapper"><button class="reactions-skeleton-el"></div></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-right"><div class="reactions-skeleton-el"></div></div></div></div><p><i>Editor's note: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander broke Wilt Chamberlain's record for most consecutive 20-point games in NBA history (127) against the </i><em><a data-clubhouse-guid="2ca761df-5f60-b2e9-22ed-e099c46d889b" href="/nba/team/_/name/bos/boston-celtics">Boston Celtics</a></em><i> on Thursday.</i></p><p>On Nov. 1, 2024, <a data-player-guid="4dcec409-3ff9-2881-2bc3-b4289ce6c36d" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4278073/shai-gilgeous-alexander">Shai Gilgeous-Alexander</a> scored 30 points in a <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/401704706/thunder-trail-blazers">137-114</a> <a data-clubhouse-guid="bd458c44-2d33-47eb-cebc-35d3d4ac595c" href="/nba/team/_/name/okc/oklahoma-city-thunder">Oklahoma City Thunder</a> win in Portland. It wasn't an especially notable performance at the time; Gilgeous-Alexander had averaged 30.1 points the previous season en route to an MVP runner-up finish.</p><p>Now, 16 months later, that game carries great historical importance, as it was the start of a 127-game odyssey for a player who has since won the <a href="/nba/story/_/id/45256670/sources-thunder-shai-gilgeous-alexander-wins-nba-mvp">regular-season MVP award</a>, been named <a href="/nba/story/_/id/45566292/thunder-shai-gilgeous-alexander-named-nba-finals-mvp">Finals MVP</a> and has now broken a <a href="/nba/story/_/id/48092442/nba-2026-2026-wilt-chamberlain-record-shai-gilgeous-alexander-okc">63-year-old record</a>.</p><p>Against the visiting Celtics, Gilgeous-Alexander scored at least 20 points for the 127th game in a row, dating to that night in Portland. He passed the great <a data-player-guid="ada3cdc4-8e3d-d94a-e117-30470c75464e" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4142/wilt-chamberlain">Wilt Chamberlain</a> for the longest such streak in NBA history.</p><p>It's a fitting record, based <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ioz01-Iah7I">in historic consistency</a>, for the man who once declared, "My whole life is consistent, everything I do."</p><p>To commemorate that consistency, here are the 20 wildest, most extreme and most impressive stats about Gilgeous-Alexander's historic 20-point streak. <em>(Note: The facts that follow reflect his performance through 126 games.) </em></p><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/i/infographics/20170710_nfl_fpr/charts/_end_rule.png" width="100%"><p><strong>1.</strong> The first surprise about Gilgeous-Alexander's accomplishment is that he even came close to Chamberlain's record. Nobody else had done so previously; before SGA, the second-longest 20-point streak in NBA history belonged to Chamberlain himself, at 92 games. There's a reason <a href="/nba/story/_/id/48092442/nba-2026-2026-wilt-chamberlain-record-shai-gilgeous-alexander-okc">Gilgeous-Alexander considers Chamberlain</a> "almost like a mythical creature," because his statistical feats were so singular.</p><p>Oscar Robertson's 79-gamer was in third place -- which means that for all of NBA history until this season, Chamberlain was the only player to push his streak as long as the equivalent of an entire season. And even Robertson's streak brought him only 63% of the way to the record.</p><p><strong>2.</strong> The competition has been even less compelling recently. In the 21st century, <a data-player-guid="dc324fde-eee8-98e2-09ac-d31d8ad9909e" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3202/kevin-durant">Kevin Durant</a> is the only other player to get more than halfway to Chamberlain; he had a 72-gamer that started in his last season in Oklahoma City and ended early in his Warriors tenure. Next on the 21st-century list is Kobe Bryant, who reached 63 games in a row -- exactly halfway to Chamberlain's 126 -- from December 2005 to November 2006.</p><p>On average, the longest 20-point streak for every MVP this century other than Gilgeous-Alexander is only 36 games.</p><p align="center"><strong>Longest 20-point streaks from 21st-century MVPs</strong></p><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/photo/2026/0310/r1626378_608x342_16-9.png" width="100%"><p><strong>3.</strong> Meanwhile, the next-longest active streak behind SGA belongs to <a data-player-guid="87ff907c-c68c-a82d-1215-37e05d0c62ef" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/6450/kawhi-leonard">Kawhi Leonard</a> at 42 games, and Leonard and the currently injured <a data-player-guid="89410119-0011-5ba0-efed-7b0b6c756c23" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3059318/joel-embiid">Joel Embiid</a> (24 games) are the only active players whose streaks extend back to 2025, let alone 2024. Gilgeous-Alexander's streak isn't the result of an era effect so much as a superlative achievement from the reigning MVP -- and the heavy favorite to repeat in 2025-26.</p><p><strong>4.</strong> Against that historical backdrop, it's worth doing some math to place the extremity of this streak in context. Over the past two seasons, during which Gilgeous-Alexander embarked on his record-setting mission, players named to an All-Star team have scored 20-plus points in 71% of their games. Given that base probability, the odds of an All-Star reaching 20-plus points 126 games in a row are about 1 in 3,200,000,000,000,000,000, or 1 in 3.2 quintillion.</p><p>The quintillion range is also the same magnitude as one estimate of the <a href="https://www.npr.org/sections/krulwich/2012/09/17/161096233/which-is-greater-the-number-of-sand-grains-on-earth-or-stars-in-the-sky">number of grains of sand on Earth</a>. In other words, the chances of a modern NBA All-Star surpassing Chamberlain's streak are roughly the odds of finding a specific grain of sand somewhere on the entire planet.</p><p><strong>5.</strong> So how, exactly, did Gilgeous-Alexander beat those outrageous odds and chase down one of Chamberlain's many long-standing records? One answer is that he excelled from every area on the court.</p><p>Splitting Gilgeous-Alexander's 4,092 points during his streak across the general regions of the offensive end, as defined by GeniusIQ, there is a remarkably even distribution. He has scored between 16% and 25% of his points from all five areas: free throw line, restricted area, key, midrange and 3-point range.</p><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/photo/2026/0310/r1626380_600x400_3-2.png" width="100%"><p>For comparison, the second-highest-scoring player over the past two seasons is <a data-player-guid="583794eb-0f38-9bbd-3e25-9dd33b7f83b8" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3945274/luka-doncic">Luka Doncic</a>. And while Doncic generates a similar proportion of his points from the charity stripe (23%), he is far more skewed to 3-point range (36%) than closer to the basket (11% from the restricted area). Gilgeous-Alexander's shot distribution is uniquely egalitarian for a guard in the modern NBA.</p><p><strong>6.</strong> Gilgeous-Alexander is also not a static player; he has improved during his streak. He is only a 35.9% career 3-point shooter, but he's up at 39.3% since last year's All-Star break. That mark places him in the same range as star shooters <a data-player-guid="5dda51f1-50c9-66e1-2026-400b73f34fad" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3975/stephen-curry">Stephen Curry</a> (39.8%), <a data-player-guid="87ff907c-c68c-a82d-1215-37e05d0c62ef" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/6450/kawhi-leonard">Kawhi Leonard</a> (39.0%), <a data-player-guid="a2edb458-a371-3fba-ba5a-b24dd2fd2d9e" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4594268/anthony-edwards">Anthony Edwards</a> (38.9%), <a data-player-guid="10c8715d-6e1c-40de-59cb-625bf16ebbc7" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4066320/desmond-bane">Desmond Bane</a> (38.8%), and <a data-player-guid="3411530a-7ab7-e8dc-e4f1-65d59a559520" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/6475/klay-thompson">Klay Thompson</a> (38.4%) during that span.</p><p><strong>7.</strong> Gilgeous-Alexander has also improved inside the arc: He's making a career-high 60.1% of his 15 2-point attempts per game this season. That's the most efficient 2-point performance for a guard in NBA history (minimum 10 attempts per game).</p><p><strong>8.</strong> Gilgeous-Alexander's overall efficiency is also near the top of the charts thanks to the combination of his improved 3-point marksmanship, historic 2-point accuracy and penchant for drawing fouls (and converting at a 90% clip from the line). With a 66.7% true shooting mark, Gilgeous-Alexander ranks second on the all-time list of 30-point scorers. Only Stephen Curry's 66.9% figure from 2015-16 -- when he won a unanimous MVP award and produced arguably the greatest offensive season in NBA history, with a record-setting 402 3-pointers -- ranks higher.</p><p><strong>9.</strong> Gilgeous-Alexander's overall efficiency improves further because he's not just generating positive plays, but avoiding negative ones as well. He's averaging just 2.1 turnovers per game this season, which is the fewest on record for a player averaging 30 PPG. The previous record belonged to ... Gilgeous-Alexander, who averaged 2.2 turnovers in 2023-24. And last season, he was at 2.4 turnovers per game, which is tied with Michael Jordan's 1995-96 campaign for the third-fewest.</p><p><strong>10.</strong> Since the start of last season, Gilgeous-Alexander naturally leads the league in 20-point games. But he is also No. 1 by a wide margin in 30-point games, with 86. Doncic is in second place with 59, which is 31% behind SGA's total</p><p><strong>11.</strong> Gilgeous-Alexander is also tied for the most 40-point games since the start of last season; he and Edwards have 18 apiece.</p><p><strong>12. </strong>And even though Gilgeous-Alexander is known more as a consistent scorer than an explosive one, he also leads the league in 50-point games since the start of last season, with five. <a data-player-guid="22f57511-06ae-1587-f01b-33965365ce0d" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3112335/nikola-jokic">Nikola Jokic</a> (four) is the only other player with more than two.</p><p><strong>13.</strong> Plotting Gilgeous-Alexander's game-by-game point totals over the course of his 126-game streak reveals another even spread. Gilgeous-Alexander has scored exactly 20 points, 21 points, 22 points and so on, all the way up to 42, at least once.</p><p align="center"><strong>SGA's single-game point totals during streak</strong></p><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/photo/2026/0310/r1626376_608x342_16-9.png" width="100%"><p>He is most often clustered in the low 30s. His modal point total during his streak is 30 points on the nose (13 times), followed by 31 (11 times), 35 (nine times) and 32 and 33 (eight times apiece).</p><p><strong>14.</strong> Those point totals pale in comparison to Chamberlain's, of course. The Big Dipper averaged 49.2 PPG over his record streak, compared to SGA's 32.5. But Chamberlain also benefited from now-unheard-of playing time, as he averaged 48.4 minutes per contest and was subbed out in just three of 126 games. (In those games, he still played 45, 40 and 36 minutes.)</p><p>Gilgeous-Alexander, for comparison, hasn't reached 48 minutes in any game during his streak and has far more games with minutes totals in the 20s (24) than 40s (five).</p><p><strong>15.</strong> Calculating the two stars' stats on a per-36-minute basis to even out the playing time disparity reveals a much closer competition: 36.6 points per 36 minutes for Chamberlain versus 34.4 from Gilgeous-Alexander.</p><p><strong>16.</strong> One statistic where the two scoring superstars notably diverge is team success. Oklahoma City has gone 102-24 during Gilgeous-Alexander's streak, compared to the Philadelphia/San Francisco Warriors' 66-60 mark during Chamberlain's.</p><p><strong>17. </strong>Zooming out, Gilgeous-Alexander is on pace to average 30-plus PPG for the fourth consecutive season. The only other players in NBA history to accomplish this feat are Chamberlain (seven straight years), Jordan (seven), Robertson (four) and Adrian Dantley (four).</p><p><strong>18. </strong>The only players to average 30-plus PPG for four straight years and win a title during that span are Gilgeous-Alexander and Jordan.</p><aside class="inline editorial float-r" data-behavior="article_related"><h2 class="editorial-title">Editor's Picks</h2><ul><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48155517/nba-2025-2026-alternate-reality-kevin-durant-2021-eastern-conference-giannis-kyrie-irving" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0309/nba_kd_alternate_reality2_cr_1x1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48155517/nba-2025-2026-alternate-reality-kevin-durant-2021-eastern-conference-giannis-kyrie-irving">How the NBA would've changed if KD's sneaker were smaller: An alternate timeline with new champs and wild trades</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Zach Kram</span></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48165009/2026-nba-mock-draft-top-30-first-round-picks-march-madness" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0310/260311_nba-mock-draft_1x1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48165009/2026-nba-mock-draft-top-30-first-round-picks-march-madness">Updated 2026 NBA mock draft: Who's rising and falling ahead of March Madness?</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Jeremy Woo</span></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48169597/celebrate-bam-adebayo-83-point-game-debate-merits-miami-heat-kobe-stat-chasing" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0311/r1626682_1296x1296_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48169597/celebrate-bam-adebayo-83-point-game-debate-merits-miami-heat-kobe-stat-chasing">'It will be something we talk about forever': The karmic power of Bam Adebayo's 83-point game</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Brian Windhorst</span></div></h2></li></ul><div class="button button-alt sm btn-related">2 Related</div></aside><p><strong>19.</strong> So, returning to Gilgeous-Alexander's present accomplishment: How high can his 20-point streak climb? After all, Chamberlain's streak snapped because of a fluke rather than underperformance: After 126 20-point games in a row, he was ejected just four minutes into Game 127 when he received two technical fouls for arguing a foul call against a teammate. Immediately thereafter, Chamberlain eclipsed the 20-point mark for 20 consecutive games, then missed once, then embarked on his 92-game streak.</p><p>In other words, Chamberlain was only one ill-timed argument and a few bounces away from an astonishing 240-game 20-point streak, which would have nearly doubled the actual record.</p><div class="ad-300"><div class="ad-center"><div class="ad-slot ad-slot-incontent2 ad-wrapper" data-slot-type="incontent2" data-slot-kvps="pos=incontent2" data-independent="true"></div></div></div><p><strong>20.</strong> Gilgeous-Alexander, conversely, isn't a threat to be ejected, and he hasn't even had any close calls of late. Since he returned from injury at the end of February, Gilgeous-Alexander has averaged 30.8 PPG and scored at least 26 in all five games.</p><p>It's still reasonable to expect his streak to end before too long, just given the sheer odds against even reaching triple digits, let alone continuing in perpetuity. Before his current run, SGA's longest 20-point streaks were 37 games in 2023, 29 games in 2024 and 20 games in 2023-24. He has clearly leveled up as a scorer over the past two seasons, but plenty of other historically great scorers didn't get anywhere near 126 games -- and counting -- in a row.</p><p>But that's all the more reason to appreciate Gilgeous-Alexander's latest achievement. Matching any of Chamberlain's multitudinous records is worthy of celebration.</p>
</div><footer class="article-footer" data-behavior="footer_region"><div class="sponsored-links"></div>

href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4066261/bam-adebayo">Bam Adebayo</a>'s 83-point outing, the <a data-clubhouse-guid="81e3212c-30ef-9b1b-5edb-453b13ff265a" href="/nba/team/_/name/mia/miami-heat">Miami Heat</a> star's former AAU coach, Ty White, is stuck in a wild mental dichotomy as he ponders the historic night.</p><aside class="inline editorial float-r" data-behavior="article_related"><h2 class="editorial-title">Editor's Picks</h2><ul><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48169366/bam-adebayo-83-points-social-reaction-lebron-james-aja-wilson">'BAM': LeBron, A'ja lead reaction to Bam Adebayo's 83-point game</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Brianna Williams</span></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48168997/bam-adebayo-scores-83-second-most-points-nba-history" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0311/r1626561_683x683_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48168997/bam-adebayo-scores-83-second-most-points-nba-history">'Wilt, me, then Kobe': Adebayo scores 83 points</a><div class="editorial--metadata"></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48169597/celebrate-bam-adebayo-83-point-game-debate-merits-miami-heat-kobe-stat-chasing" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0311/r1626682_1296x1296_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48169597/celebrate-bam-adebayo-83-point-game-debate-merits-miami-heat-kobe-stat-chasing">'It will be something we talk about forever': The karmic power of Bam Adebayo's 83-point game</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Brian Windhorst</span></div></h2></li></ul><div class="button button-alt sm btn-related">2 Related</div></aside><p>Yes, he's shocked that the 6-foot-9 center finished with the mind-boggling stat line, but not because he didn't think he was capable.</p><p>It's just that 83 points is, well, 83 points!</p><p>"I mean, it's so crazy to think that anyone could score that many points in an NBA game," said White, director of Team Loaded. "But in retrospect, the kid was always a super athlete who always had a nose for the basketball. I wasn't even watching the game live, so many people started texting me saying, 'Bam Bam Bam!'"</p><p>Adebayo's 83 points, nine rebounds, three assists, two steals and two blocks came in a <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/401810793/wizards-heat">150-129 </a>win over the <a data-clubhouse-guid="64d73af6-b8ec-e213-87e8-a4eab3a692e7" href="/nba/team/_/name/wsh/washington-wizards">Washington Wizards</a> on Tuesday night. He went 7-of-22 from 3 and set an NBA record for most free throws made (36) and attempted (43).</p><p>Prior to the game, Adebayo's career high was 41 points.</p><p>Adebayo's 83 points were the second-most points scored in a game in NBA history, trailing only <a data-player-guid="ada3cdc4-8e3d-d94a-e117-30470c75464e" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/4142/wilt-chamberlain">Wilt Chamberlain</a>'s legendary 100-point outing in 1962.</p><blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">83 points. The bucket that made HISTORY <a href="https://t.co/qgF59KhZEv">https://t.co/qgF59KhZEv</a> <a href="https://t.co/9kEVit5zuU">pic.twitter.com/9kEVit5zuU</a></p>— Miami HEAT (@MiamiHEAT) <a href="https://twitter.com/MiamiHEAT/status/2031554375370359127?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">March 11, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script><p>White said that, aside from Adebayo's overwhelming athleticism, he had the innate ability to "do something to 'wow' you every game" during his playing days with Team Loaded.</p><p>"That was without fail," White said. "He would do something that would make you just scratch your head at how he was able to do that. We were the most exciting team in the country to watch, and it was mostly because of him."</p><p>White pointed to Adebayo's development as a scorer over the years, going from a powerful athlete to a meticulously skilled scorer.</p><p>"When he was with me, he was more of a lob threat, drop-off guy," White said. "Now, he's initiating offense and he's one of the most versatile players in the league. The way he's evolved as a player is just unreal."</p><p>That said, White's amazement at the point total is understandable -- Adebayo's career high with Team Loaded was just 30 points. He averaged 25 points and 15 rebounds a game.</p><p>"Eighty-three is just wild, I don't care who you are," White said. "He's got to just believe the sky is the limit after that. His confidence has to be at an all-time high right now and rightfully so. People can say what they want, but at the end of the day he's No. 2 behind Wilt with 83, and nobody can take that away."</p>

Butler/NBAE via Getty Images</span></figure><div class="article-body"><div class="article-meta"><div class="byline-wrap"><ul class="authors byline-authors"><li class="single-author mugshot-shown"><div class="author-img"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/i/columnists/full/windhorst_brian.png&h=80&w=80&scale=crop" alt="" width="40" height="40"></div><div class="author has-bio">Brian Windhorst<span class="timestamp ">Mar 19, 2026, 07:00 AM ET</span></div><div class="author-overlay"><a class="icon-font-after icon-close-solid-after" href="#">Close</a><ul><li>ESPN.com NBA writer since 2010</li>

content-reactions-popup"></div></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-loading"><div class="reactions-skeleton-left"><div class="reactions-skeleton-el share-skeleton"></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-el"></div></div><div class="reactions-skeleton-right"><div class="reactions-skeleton-el"></div></div></div></div><p><a data-player-guid="1f6592b3-ff53-d321-8dc5-6038d48c1786" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/1966/lebron-james">LeBron James</a> took a deep pull from a banana milkshake from Swensons, an Akron-based restaurant chain and a special postgame catering request the <a data-clubhouse-guid="81e3212c-30ef-9b1b-5edb-453b13ff265a" href="/nba/team/_/name/mia/miami-heat">Miami Heat</a> made a standard order whenever they were in town. As he finished, James flopped back in his chair after a long, strange night.</p><p>"This was one of the most bizarre, unique days of my life," he said, half smiling and half sighing.</p><p>It was approaching midnight on March 20, 2013, and James had just experienced one of the most memorable, historic and dominant moments of his career.</p><p>The Heat, in the prime of the Heatles phenomenon, had just won their <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278729/heat-cavaliers">24th consecutive game</a> after a 27-point second-half comeback in <a href="/nba/team/_/name/cle/cleveland-cavaliers">Cleveland</a>.</p><p>There are arguments to be made about when James was at the peak of his career.</p><p>Some might favor the <a href="/nba/story/_/page/playoffindex16/nba-playoffs-coverage">2016 Finals</a>, when he led the Cavaliers, down 3-1, to a stunning <a href="/nba/playoffs/2016/story/_/id/16390925/nba-playoffs-2016-how-lebron-james-cleveland-cavaliers-lifted-curse">seven-game upset</a> over <a data-player-guid="5dda51f1-50c9-66e1-2026-400b73f34fad" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3975/stephen-curry">Stephen Curry</a> and the 73-win <a data-clubhouse-guid="77a8546f-24bf-a333-8161-bf9f75396c49" href="/nba/team/_/name/gs/golden-state-warriors">Golden State Warriors</a>. Others might point to a breathtaking run during the 2018 playoffs, the last of an incredible streak of eight straight trips to the Finals.</p><p>But the winter and early spring of 2013 has its place, too. At age 28 with his athleticism at full power, his maturing skill development and the Heat designing a roster and system around him that allowed him to fully bloom, James and his team were <i>devastating</i> the league.</p><p>Whenever James returns to Miami and the Kaseya Center, as he will Thursday night with the <a data-clubhouse-guid="2876e98b-b9bc-2920-4319-46e6943f8be4" href="/nba/team/_/name/lal/los-angeles-lakers">Los Angeles Lakers</a>, unsure of his future and whether this might be his final visit, the memory of the <a href="/nba/playoffs/2013/story/_/id/9409188/2013-nba-finals-lebron-james-named-nba-finals-mvp">2013 title</a> he won in the building is never far from his mind.</p><p>And at the core of those memories is the <a href="/blog/truehoop/miamiheat/post/_/id/17156/the-top-10-moments-of-miamis-win-streak">27-game winning streak</a> the Heat assembled that season, the second longest in NBA history.</p><p>Now 13 years later, the tales from that stretch have aged like one of James' favorite fine red wines.</p><hr><h2>Feb. 3, 2013: Win No. 1</h2><p>It was a frigid Sunday afternoon in Toronto, a notorious situation that for decades had caught numerous teams in letdowns. But coming off a disappointing loss to the rival <a data-clubhouse-guid="547fc042-3e02-4795-9637-9ab84322b625" href="/nba/team/_/name/ind/indiana-pacers">Indiana Pacers</a> two days earlier, the second time that season the Pacers had beaten them, the Heat were in no mood for such a performance.</p><p>James, <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/1987/dwyane-wade">Dwyane Wade</a> and <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/1977/chris-bosh">Chris Bosh</a> combined for 81 points on 60% shooting in the <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278427/heat-raptors">100-85 victory</a>. It was the start of what would be a stretch of mind-numbingly and ruthlessly efficient games.</p><p>It was <em>also</em> Super Bowl Sunday.</p><aside class="inline editorial float-r" data-behavior="article_related"><h2 class="editorial-title">Editor's Picks</h2><ul><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48246814/lebron-james-6-dunks-vs-rockets-key-lakers-7th-straight-win" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0319/r1630580_1296x1296_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:1" href="/nba/story/_/id/48246814/lebron-james-6-dunks-vs-rockets-key-lakers-7th-straight-win">LeBron's 6 dunks key Lakers' 7th straight win</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Tim MacMahon</span></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48215728/glass-my-hand-los-angeles-lakers-guard-marcus-smart-return-punch" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0315/r1628871_1296x1296_1-1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:2" href="/nba/story/_/id/48215728/glass-my-hand-los-angeles-lakers-guard-marcus-smart-return-punch">'Glass in my hand': Inside Marcus Smart's incredible return from The Punch</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">Dave McMenamin</span></div></h2></li><li><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48185582/nba-2025-2026-regular-season-final-month-giannis-jayson-tatum-more" class="img-link"><img src="https://a.espncdn.com/combiner/i?img=/photo/2026/0316/nba_last-month-season_1x1.jpg&w=130&h=130&scale=crop&location=center" width="65" height="65"></a><h2><a name="&lpos=story:editorspicks:inline:3" href="/nba/story/_/id/48185582/nba-2025-2026-regular-season-final-month-giannis-jayson-tatum-more">Biggest questions, projections and more: Storylines to watch ahead of NBA playoffs</a><div class="editorial--metadata"><span class="author">NBA Insiders</span></div></h2></li></ul><div class="button button-alt sm btn-related">2 Related</div></aside><p>The <a data-clubhouse-guid="38248455-3da4-84e4-8090-98b5cc38ffdc" href="/nfl/team/_/name/bal/baltimore-ravens">Baltimore Ravens</a> and <a data-clubhouse-guid="985a261f-296c-c95c-32a5-addc4df75001" href="/nfl/team/_/name/sf/san-francisco-49ers">San Francisco 49ers</a> were facing off in <a href="/nfl/game/_/gameId/330203025/ravens-49ers">Super Bowl XLVII</a>, and the Heat were in a pinch. Their charter plane, after clearing customs, likely wouldn't have been able to get airborne until kickoff. At the time, the plane didn't have live TV or Wi-Fi and everyone would be in the dark for the three hours going back to Miami, missing most of the game.</p><p>But team officials had a surprise. They delayed the flight and rented out a section at the famous Toronto sports bar Real Sports, next door to the Raptors' arena, where there is a 40-foot-wide screen. And there was an open bar too.</p><p>The beer flowed and Heat forward <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/976/shane-battier">Shane Battier</a> got drunk on Anchor Steam, stealing a <a href="https://www.espn.com/nfl/player/_/id/14001/colin-kaepernick">Colin Kaepernick</a> jersey off a dummy set up for the occasion. Later, on the way to the airport, Battier grabbed the microphone on the bus and gave an impromptu speech to his lubed-up teammates to "<a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GFvbTI05ms4">touch the people</a>" as he urged them to take advantage of the Heat's popularity in that time and place.</p><p>"I was feeling the love. It was one of the great days of being a teammate," Battier said on "The OGs" show in 2024. "I just felt like someone had to say something to capture the moment."</p><p>There would be no loss for the next 52 days.</p><hr><h2>Feb. 26, 2013: Win No. 12</h2><p>Knowing the big three Heat stars would have to sacrifice shots when playing together, coach Erik Spoelstra sold them on a multiyear plan to emphasize efficiency.</p><p>Take higher-value, higher-percentage and higher-leverage shots, he told them, and their scoring wouldn't fall off.</p><p>It all came together during this run -- almost <em>too</em> well.</p><p>The ball movement and defense-to-offense transition was clicking, and James, Bosh and Wade relentlessly hunted for only great shots -- to the point where sometimes the bench players would yell at them for passing up shots that were merely "good."</p><p>During one six-game stretch in the streak, James made <i>72%</i> of his shots while averaging 31 points. In a nine-game stretch, Wade made 61% of his shots and averaged 28 points. And in a six-game stretch, Bosh made 65% of his shots and averaged 23.</p><p>It culminated in this <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278565/kings-heat">double-OT 141-129 win</a> over the <a data-clubhouse-guid="e266c4ea-9af3-1bd8-06fb-fd608fd30e26" href="/nba/team/_/name/sac/sacramento-kings">Sacramento Kings</a>. James scored 40 points on 23 shots with a then-career-high 16 assists. Wade had 39 points on 28 shots. It was their last game of February. James shot 64% for the month.</p><p>The next day, the Heat secretly filmed their viral <a href="https://www.facebook.com/reel/247693670307666/">Harlem Shake video</a>, refusing to tell coaches and staff what they were doing after they brought in a bunch of costumes and props and then holed up in the locker room.</p><p>The quirky response to the trend of the moment got millions of hits over the next week, solidifying even further that everything the team did at that point turned to gold.</p><p>"In practice that day we were giddy," Wade remembered in a recent Amazon Prime broadcast. "We were in there like little kids, giggling. Spo was mad at us because we were giggling too much."</p><hr><h2>March 6, 2013: Win No. 16</h2><p>With less than a minute left and holding a three-point lead and the ball, the <a data-clubhouse-guid="74c23102-8c27-c8e6-ec1b-8a8fd4d42554" href="/nba/team/_/name/orl/orlando-magic">Orlando Magic</a> thought they were going to put an end to the streak. It had been a statement game from young big man <a data-player-guid="2c9b229f-cbd5-8e0b-cf3a-236f74f23867" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/6478/nikola-vucevic">Nikola Vucevic</a>, who had 25 points and 21 rebounds.</p><p>But the Heat's defense tightened when it mattered most, finishing the game with five consecutive stops. It set up a winner from James, who hit a driving layup with 3.2 seconds left to save the streak and win <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278623/magic-heat">97-96</a>.</p><p>James had 24 points, the 200th straight regular-season game in which he had scored in double figures. He would do so 1,097 more times over the next 12½ years.</p><p>"He is defined by winning plays," Spoelstra said that night. "He just continues to make them."</p><hr><h2>March 18, 2013: Win No. 23</h2><p>The Heat and <a data-clubhouse-guid="2ca761df-5f60-b2e9-22ed-e099c46d889b" href="/nba/team/_/name/bos/boston-celtics">Celtics</a>, deep into their rivalry by this point, made for appointment viewing, especially the games in Boston. Though they'd beaten the Celtics in the playoffs the previous two seasons, the Heat hadn't won a regular-season game in Boston in six years.</p><p>The Celtics were missing <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3026/rajon-rondo">Rajon Rondo</a>, who had just <a href="/nba/story/_/id/8887106/rajon-rondo-boston-celtics-torn-acl-undergo-surgery">torn the ACL</a> in his right knee, and <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/261/kevin-garnett">Kevin Garnett</a>, who was hampered by a thigh injury and the flu.</p><p>In their absence, <a data-player-guid="be3d61b0-85b4-0c0b-af38-8002721290b9" href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/3209/jeff-green">Jeff Green</a> had one of the best games of his career, scoring 43 points. With eight minutes to go, Boston had a 13-point lead and the jubilant crowd at TD Garden was itching to celebrate a victory.</p><p>But James wouldn't allow it, leading an improbable comeback from 17 points down to finish with 37 points, seven rebounds and 12 assists. He scored 13 in the fourth quarter, tying the game with a clutch layup and then delivering the winning 20-footer with 10.5 seconds left. Final score: <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278718/heat-celtics">Heat 105, Celtics 103</a>.</p><p>He also made a massive highlight that would become one of the <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PZTGCOjsKE8">defining moments</a> of his career, dunking over personal rival <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/841/jason-terry">Jason Terry</a>. Terry had needled and then gotten the better of James during the <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/310612014/mavericks-heat">2011 Finals</a> when Terry was with the victorious <a data-clubhouse-guid="f00d1f4e-4ce6-d581-466c-5b52531cf7ad" href="/nba/team/_/name/dal/dallas-mavericks">Dallas Mavericks</a>.</p><p>James relished <a href="/nba/truehoop/miamiheat/story/_/id/9075039/lebron-james-miami-heat-says-posterizing-boston-celtics-jason-terry-was-pleasure">putting Terry on a poster</a>. In a rare move, James <a href="/blog/boston/celtics/post/_/id/4703428/james-on-terry-dunk-glad-it-happened">stood over Terry</a> as he lay on the court, with veteran referee Scott Foster stepping in and giving James a technical foul for taunting.</p><p>"That it happened to JT made it that much sweeter. Because we all know JT and he talks too much sometimes," James said then. "I'm glad it happened to him."</p><p>And then the Heat returned to Cleveland.</p><hr><h2>March 20, 2013: Win No. 24</h2><p>With seven minutes left in the third quarter, the Cavaliers took a 27-point lead on the Heat.</p><p>Three years post-"<a href="/nba/story/_/id/29375065/the-decision-high-stakes-maneuvers-lebron-james-dwyane-wade-chris-bosh">Decision</a>," the fans in Cleveland, infamous for the venom they'd aimed at James upon his departure, were reveling in the chance to hand him a loss that would end the Heat's chances of catching the Lakers' 33-game streak from 1971-72.</p><p>But suddenly, Miami got hot from 3-point range. Battier hit three of them. <a href="https://www.espn.com/nba/player/_/id/9/ray-allen">Ray Allen</a> hit two. The Cavs couldn't make a shot or get a defensive rebound. James opened the fourth quarter by nailing three straight 3-pointers of his own, and the Heat finished off a 45-12 run that flipped the game.</p><p>Then, with eight minutes left, a young man sprinted past security and onto the floor. Wearing a <a href="/nba/story/_/id/11588922/the-three-year-plan-lure-lebron-james-back-cleveland">white T-shirt</a> with the words "We Miss You" on the front and "Come Back 2014" on the back, he positioned himself in front of James, who was scheduled to be a free agent the following year.</p><p>Heat vice president of team security David Holcombe grabbed the man, James Blair, within seconds. But James stopped Holcombe as he was taking Blair off the floor and patted the fan on the head.</p><p>Blair, a lifelong James fan, told him to "come home" as he was being <a href="/nba/story/_/id/11570860/cleveland-cavaliers-lift-ban-lebron-james-fan-rushed-court">dragged off the floor</a> by police. The next day, after he was released from jail, Blair had been both followed by James on social media and <a href="/nba/story?src=desktop&id=11570835">banned by the Cavs</a> from returning to the arena.</p><p>James finished with 25 points, 12 rebounds, 10 assists, three steals and two blocks in a <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278729/heat-cavaliers">98-95 victory</a>.</p><p>"One of the best comebacks I've ever been part of," James said before finishing his milkshake.</p><hr><p>The Heat's win streak ended a week later in Chicago, on March 27, when the <a data-clubhouse-guid="e588ccf1-ba03-ea43-c34c-9a9c8d1895ca" href="/nba/team/_/name/chi/chicago-bulls">Chicago Bulls</a> jumped out to a 10-point lead in the first quarter and prevented the Heat from going on one of their patented runs. The crowd at United Center celebrated heartily after their team held on to a <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400278782/heat-bulls">101-97 victory</a> to end the Heat's quest for history.</p><p>Only one team since has won more than 20 in a row, when the 2015-16 Warriors <a href="/blog/golden-state-warriors/post/_/id/1037/24-0-draymond-green-beats-celtic-green">opened the season 24-0</a> on the way to their record 73-win season.</p><p>The Heat were 29-14 when the streak started and finished their season on a 37-2 run. They went on to win their <a href="/nba/game/_/gameId/400467339/spurs-heat">second straight title</a> in another piece of history, their seven-game series with the <a data-clubhouse-guid="8aef8547-32f5-0943-a1de-e734567674cc" href="/nba/team/_/name/sa/san-antonio-spurs">San Antonio Spurs</a> that featured <a href="/nba/story/_/page/nbarankfinalsrayallen/best-nba-finals-games-ray-allen-rescues-miami-heat">Allen's miraculous 3-pointer</a> to send Game 6 to overtime.</p><div class="ad-300"><div class="ad-center"><div class="ad-slot ad-slot-incontent2 ad-wrapper" data-slot-type="incontent2" data-slot-kvps="pos=incontent2" data-independent="true"></div></div></div><p>It was never the same for that Heat group again -- they lost to the Spurs in a Finals rematch in 2014, and James returned to Cleveland that summer -- but the streak lives on as that team's trademark.</p><p>"I wish I had him for another 6-8 years," Heat president Pat Riley said last month, referencing James' departure a year later. "It would've been great, but we'll never know [how many titles could've been won], will we?"</p><p>James, who hugged Riley when Riley was in Los Angeles to have his statue unveiled outside Crypto.com Arena in February, cherishes that time.</p><p>"The four years that we had was great. Was able to pick up two chips," James said. "A lot of great memories. A lot of great times there."</p>
